OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 47-year-old woman presented with epigastric pain and uneasiness since six months. She had no difficulty in swallowing or regurgitation of food. She had no respiratory complaints. An ultrasonography of the abdomen was unremarkable. The patient was subjected to an erect chest radiograph for routine evaluation.
 Imaging Findings: Erect frontal chest radiograph showed a well-defined, lobulated, homogeneous, soft tissue opacity (green arrow) in the left lower hemithorax, obscuring the medial aspect of the left dome of the diaphragm. It was seen as continuous with an ill-defined, left paravertebral soft tissue opacity (pink arrow). There was no silhouetting of the left heart border (Figure 1).
 
 A barium (95% w/v) oesophagogram showed unobstructed passage of barium through the thoracic soft tissue opacity. The retrocardiac oesophagus was tortuous and deviated to the left with a normal gastro-oesophageal junction (Figure 2).
 
 An unenhanced CT of the chest was performed with oral contrast, followed by intravenous contrast-enhanced CT with oral contrast in the same sitting. Axial unenhanced images showed circumferential, homogeneous thickening of the lower oesophageal wall with patency of the lumen showing orally administered water-soluble contrast. There were a few tiny foci of calcification within the wall (pink arrow on Figure 3). Sagittal venous phase images showed homogeneous enhancement of the thickened oesophageal wall without areas of necrosis (blue arrow) or evidence of invasion into surrounding structures (Figure 4). Coronal unenhanced images in the lung window showed surrounding atelectasis without signs of lung parenchymal involvement (Figure 5).

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 66-year-old man with no significant medical history develops back pain after a running session. After starting physiotherapy, the complaints became acutely worse and did not disappear during the night. No other B-symptoms were present.
 Imaging Findings: Computer tomography (CT) of the thoracic spine showed a moth-eaten osteolytic aspect of the 7th thoracic vertebral body with a pathological wedge-shaped compression fracture. There is an associated homogeneously enhancing extensive paravertebral and epidural soft tissue component (approximately 56 x 65 x 47 mm) with encasement of the azygos vein.
 
 MRI demonstrated diffuse T1 hypointense, STIR hyperintense (compared to the disci) bone marrow infiltration of the 7th vertebral body with paravertebral and epidural mass extension. The mass showed homogeneous contrast enhancement with marked diffusion restriction (ADC value of 710 μm²/sec). A compressive myelopathy developed based on the epidural component. Encasement of the azygos vein is seen, as well as intercostal arteries traversing through the mass without invasion. There were no signs of invasion of the disci or other vertebrae.
 
 Additional CT thorax and abdomen, PET-CT, and whole body MRI showed no other lesions or adenopathies.

Clinical History: A 65-year-old lady presented with complaints of pain and swelling in the left-hand middle finger following minor trauma. She has been having on-and-off dull aching pain. The blood investigation showed mildly elevated CRP (5.8 mg/dl) and ESR (50 mm/hr) but normal serum creatinine, calcium, phosphorus and alkaline phosphatase.
 Imaging Findings: X-ray showed a well-defined central expansile lytic lesion with scalloped margins involving the base of the 3rd proximal phalanx of the left hand with few specks of calcifications, and pathological fractures involving medial and lateral walls of the 3rd proximal phalanx (Figure 1a). Computed tomography showed similar findings to X-ray (Figure 1b). Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) of the hand showed a well-defined expansile lytic lesion which was T1 hypointense, T2 hyperintense, and showed no diffusion restriction with scalloping and fracture involving medial and lateral walls, causing splaying of sagittal bands on both sides (Figures 1c, 1d, 1e, 1f, and 1g).

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 54-year-old female patient, with no relevant previous history, presented to the outpatient clinic complaining of a progressively growing swelling at the left popliteal fossa, with associated limited knee flexion and local pain. At clinical examination, a firm and non-mobile swelling was evident at the popliteal region on the left.
 Imaging Findings: MRI showed a subfascial soft tissue mass mainly composed of tissue isointense to the muscle on T1-weighted (T1) sequences, hyperintense on T2-weighted fat-suppressed (T2FS) sequences and which, after contrast with fat suppression (T1 Gd), showed intense and fast enhancement, as well as “feathery” margins (flame sign). These areas were mixed with areas of very low signal on T1 and T2FS, which did not enhance after contrast (Figures 1a, 1b, 1c, and 2). Some diffusion restriction was seen in the more enhancing areas (Figures 3a and 3b).
 
 The extra-articular mass extended into the subcutis, as well as into the muscle bellies of the biceps femoris and medial gastrocnemius. The mass also encased the peroneal nerve (Figure 4), following its trajectory until the level of the fibular head (Figures 5a and 5b). Reactive oedema along the deep fascia of the lower leg was also present.

There was no evidence of free intraperitoneal gas, bowel wall thickening, or signs of bowel obstruction. No other abnormalities were observed in the abdominal and pelvic structures, and no additional masses or lesions were noted.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 13-year-old male presented with pain in the right hypochondrium and loss of appetite for 1 month. There was no history of fever. The patient has a history of a successfully treated abdominal tuberculosis, for which he had taken anti-tubercular treatment for 1 year.
 Imaging Findings: An ultrasound was performed, which showed a well-defined round to oval isoechoic lesion in segment IV of the liver, without any significant internal vascularity.
 
 A contrast CT scan was further requested, which demonstrated a well-defined round to oval lesion, measuring ~6 x 4.2 x 5.1 cm, well demarcated from the rest of the liver with the same attenuation as that of the normal liver tissue. It was located inferior to segment IV (Figures 1a and 1b) with arterial supply from a branch of the left hepatic artery (Figure 2) and venous drainage into a branch of the middle hepatic vein. It was seen to cause mild dilatation of central and peripheral intrahepatic bile ducts in the left lobe due to compression (Figure 3). The liver was otherwise normal in size with maintained segmental morphology and normal hepatic veins. The left branch of the portal vein was attenuated in calibre; however, it showed normal contrast opacification (Figure 4). Physiological areas of altered attenuation were also noted in the right lobe of the liver.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: Incidental finding in the right iliac bone of a 68-year-old male patient with lung cancer who was undergoing a routine follow-up.
 Imaging Findings: The patient had lung cancer, and during a follow-up oncology CT scan, we incidentally discovered a painless mass in his right pelvic wing. CT of the pelvis shows a well-defined lesion in the right pelvis protruding into the iliac muscle with dense calcifications and a rim of fatty tissue that confirms its lipomatous nature (Figures 1a and 1b). On bone window, the calcified nature of the lesion can be clearly seen as an excrescence from the right iliac wing, causing a mild periosteal reaction on the adjacent bone cortex (Figure 2). The scout view of the CT is also helpful in observing the lesion, its close relation to the right iliac wing, with clumped calcifications and fatty tissue in the periphery (Figure 3).

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 68-year-old former smoker with hypertension, type 2 diabetes, and hypothyroidism consulted for progressive exertional dyspnoea. Pulmonary function tests showed a restrictive pattern and reduced diffusing capacity of the lung for carbon monoxide (DLCO).
 Imaging Findings: High-resolution computed tomography (HRCT) of the chest with axial (Figures 1a and 1b), coronal (Figure 1c), and sagittal (Figure 1d) reconstructions demonstrated findings consistent with an interstitial lung disease. Bilateral involvement with a clear apicobasal gradient with extensive subpleural reticulation, traction bronchiectasis, and predominant honeycombing in the lower lobes was observed. Exuberant honeycombing was present, involving nearly the entire fibrotic pulmonary parenchyma (Figure 1b). A sharp and abrupt transition between normal and fibrotic lung parenchyma was noted in the craniocaudal direction (arrows in Figures 1c and 1d). The small areas of fibrosis of the upper lobes were limited to the anterior segments (arrowheads in Figures 1a and 1d).
 
 No evidence of superimposed infection or ground-glass opacities was revealed. No suspicious pulmonary nodules, masses, or mediastinal lymphadenopathy were identified. Mild dilation of the pulmonary arteries and a cardiac pacemaker were noted. No pleural or pericardial effusion was present.

 Imaging Findings: On chest radiographs (Figures 1a and 1b), a small ill-defined opacity was perceived in left anterior juxtacardiac topography, associated with a small left-sided pleural effusion.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A young male presents with ataxic gait, numbness in lower limbs, and difficulty in standing and walking. To evaluate further, MR brain and MR spine screening was done, which suggests compensated hydrocephalus. Ventriculoperitoneal (VP) shunting was done to treat this. After VP shunting, he presents with headache, with symmetrical weakness in the upper limbs and lower limbs.
 Imaging Findings: Pre-shunt
 
 There is prominence of the midline cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) space in between the lower parts of the bilateral cerebellar hemispheres inferior to the vermis, displacing the vermis mildly upward and the communication to the 4th ventricle—this may represent a Blake’s pouch cyst, an incidental finding as seen in Figure 1. There is moderate dilatation of both supra and infratentorial ventricular systems without obvious periventricular ooze, i.e., the possibility of compensated hydrocephalus as seen in Figure 3. There is evidence of engorgement of the epidural venous plexus causing mass effect over the cord, as seen in Figures 4a and 4b.
 
 Post-shunting
 
 There is evidence of enlargement of the ventral epidural space showing enhancement in the cervical spine and proximal dorsal spine up to the D2 level, with extension cranially till the foramen magnum with prominent flow voids, likely engorged epidural veins within it, causing cord compression as seen in Figure 5. There is evidence of an enlarged pituitary gland (mid sagittal height of 12.7 mm) as seen in Figure 6. There is evidence of a round dural venous sinus as seen in Figure 7. Given these findings, the possibility of excessive CSF diversion needs consideration.

She subsequently underwent a total abdominal hysterectomy combined with a left salpingo-oophorectomy and right salpingectomy.  On follow-up FDG-PET/CT, the retroperitoneal masses showed no significant fluorodeoxyglucose (FDG) uptake (Figures 4a and 4b).

Clinical History: A 53-year-old male patient presented to our outpatient clinic with persistent dull pain in the right upper abdomen. Physical examination revealed a flat and soft abdomen with mild tenderness in the right upper quadrant.
 Imaging Findings: The patient had complete imaging data, including CT, MR, and PET-CT. All imaging examinations showed a mass in segment VII/VIII of the liver.
 
 Unenhanced CT scan showed that the lesion was low-density, with a CT value of about 23 HU, linear calcification at the edge, and mild shrinkage of the liver capsule. After enhancement, the lesion did not enhance, and the surrounding liver parenchyma was mildly enhanced in the arterial phase (Figures 1a, 1b, 1c, and 1d).
 
 The mass contains mixed low signal on T1-weighted images, hyperintense on T2-weighted images and T2-weighted fat-suppressed images (Figures 2a, 2b, 2c, and 2d). Coronal T2-weighted fat-suppressed images and coronal venous-phase T1-weighted fat-suppressed images showed that the intrahepatic bile duct beside the mass is dilated and connected to the lesion; the edge of the lesion and the adjacent bile duct showed mild enhancement (Figures 3a and 3b). Diffusion-weighted image (DWI, b = 800 mm2/s) showed a slightly higher signal (Figure 4a). The ADC map does not show restricted diffusion (Figure 4b). Similar to the CT-enhanced images, on T1-weighted enhanced arterial phase images, it can also be seen that the liver parenchyma around the lesion is slightly enhanced (Figure 4c).
 
 PET-CT showed a slight increase in FDG metabolism (Figures 5a, 5b, 5c, and 5d).

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 62-year-old male with anal swelling and bleeding of years of evolution. Physical examination revealed a mass of approximately 20 cm in perianal region, with several fistulous tracts covering anal and perianal area with purulent secretion.
 Imaging Findings: Contrast-enhanced CT showed a voluminous low-density gluteal mass with areas of internal enhancement (Figures 1a and 1b). Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) revealed a multiloculated, cauliflower-like 21 x 16 x 11cm gluteal mass. It extended through both ischioanal fossae and fistulised to the anal canal with two posterior tracts (Figures 2a, 2b, and 2c). The lesion was hypointense with respect to the muscle in T1-weighted sequences and showed high signal intensity in T2, compatible with abundant myxoid-mucinous content. Contrast-enhanced fat-suppressed T1-weighted MR imaging showed linear and papillary mesh-like enhancing areas (Figure 2d).

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 65-year-old male presented with abdominal distension and pain of 10 days’ duration. No vomiting or obstipation was seen.
 Imaging Findings: A contrast-enhanced CT (computed tomography) scan of the abdomen was performed. Small bowel loops appeared dilated with multiple air-fluid levels (Figures 1, 2a, and 2b). A transition zone was found on the right side of the urinary bladder. A closed-loop obstruction was found with distal ileal loops as content, causing indentation of the right superolateral wall of the urinary bladder (Figures 3a, 3b, and 3c). A few dilated loops show a small bowel faeces sign (luminal contents resembling colonic faecal matter with gas bubbles). The hernial sac was filled with a minimal amount of fluid. There was no abnormal enhancement of the small bowel loops within the hernial sac. No thickening of the bowel walls was noted. Large bowel loops were collapsed. The cecum appeared normal.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 45-year-old presented with dysphagia and post-prandial vomiting since one month. She had a history of accidental corrosive ingestion 20 years back, for which she was managed surgically (an elective retrosternal oesophageal bypass surgery using a gastric pull-through procedure was performed). She underwent bougie dilatation of the esophagogastric anastomosis for similar presenting symptoms.
 Imaging Findings: Frontal chest radiograph showed a fairly well-defined, round soft tissue opacity in the retrocardiac region with a broad base towards the mediastinum (Figure 1). There were no calcifications or cavitation within the opacity. The lateral border of the descending thoracic aorta was obscured by the lesion. On esophagogram, the neo-oesophagus (pulled up stomach) was seen in the right paramedian retrosternal region (Figures 2a and 2b). There was no opacification of the native oesophagus. Unenhanced chest CT showed passage of oral contrast through the neo-oesophagus into the stomach. There was a well-defined fluid-filled elongated cystic lesion (max. diameter of 6.8 cm) in the posterior mediastinum, posterior to the heart and anterior to the descending thoracic aorta. It was communicating with the narrowed proximal and mid native oesophagus (Figures 3a and 3b).

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: We report a case of a 16-year-old patient living in a rural environment. The symptomatology goes back to 3 months with diffuse abdominal pain, distension, and with vomiting and fever 3 days before his admission to the ER. Clinical examination revealed a slight dullness of the flanks and hypogastrium.
 Imaging Findings: An abdominal ultrasound was performed and revealed multiple hepatic, splenic and peritoneal cystic lesions with large intraperitoneal effusion (Figure 1). Abdominal and pelvic CT scan in axial, sagittal and coronal sections showed multiple cystic lesions adherent to the peritoneal leaflets, falciform ligament, great omentum and mesentery (Figures 2 and 4). The largest cyst is located in the left iliac fossa, measuring 55.5 x 42 mm, containing some calcifications (Figure 3), and with a large intraperitoneal effusion. The CT scan also revealed hepatic and splenic cystic lesions.

<|channel|>analysis<|message|>We need to analyze findings. 16-year-old rural, diffuse abdominal pain, cystic lesions in liver, spleen, peritoneum, mesentery, with calcifications in one cyst. Large intraperitoneal effusion.

Differential options: Mesenteric lymphangioma, Peritoneal hydatidosis, Mesenteric cyst.

We need to decide which best fits.

Hydatid disease (Echinococcus granulosus) can cause cystic lesions in liver, spleen, peritoneum, often with calcifications, daughter cysts, can disseminate to peritoneum (peritoneal hydatidosis). Rural environment supports exposure. Age 16 fits. Calcifications especially point to hydatid. Peritoneal hydatid cysts adhere to peritoneal leaflets, omentum, mesentery. Presence of multiple cysts in liver/spleen and peritoneum is typical of secondary peritoneal hydatidosis after rupture of a hepatic cyst.

Mesenteric lymphangioma: congenital lymphatic malformation, cystic, multiloculated, usually in children; can be large, chylous fluid, not calcified; does not involve liver or spleen. No calcifications. So less likely.

Mesenteric cyst: generic term includes many etiologies, can be simple cysts, duplication cysts, etc. Usually solitary, not calcified, and liver/spleen involvement not typical. So less likely.

Thus peritoneal hydatidosis seems best.

We need to evaluate candidate pass/fail.

Mesenteric lymphangioma: Pros: age (children), cystic lesions, mesenteric involvement. Cons: absence of calcifications, involvement of liver/spleen uncommon, typical chylous fluid, no calcified walls.

Peritoneal hydatidosis: Pros: multiple cystic lesions in liver, spleen, peritoneum; calcifications; rural exposure; adherence to peritoneal structures; young patient possible. Cons: Rare but plausible.

Mesenteric cyst: Pros: cystic lesions in mesentery. Cons: does not explain hepatic/splenic cysts, calcifications.

Thus ranking: 1) Peritoneal hydatidosis, 2) Mesenteric lymphangioma, 3) Mesenteric cyst.

Tie-break: Pathognomonic calcifications in cyst + hepatic involvement indicates hydatid.

Therefore final answer: Peritoneal hydatidosis.<|end|><|start|>assistant<|channel|>final<|message|>Peritoneal hydatidosis<|return|>

Clinical History: A 71-year-old female presented with progressively worsening holocranial headache over 3 years, occasionally accompanied by photophobia and phonophobia, sometimes waking her at night, and refractory to mild analgesics. She denied visual symptoms, sinus surgery, or head trauma. Ear, nose, and throat (ENT) examination was unremarkable.
 Imaging Findings: CT of the paranasal sinuses revealed complete opacification of the left sphenoid sinus by a hyperattenuating lesion, with moderate expansion and smooth thinning and remodelling of its osseous walls (Figure 1a). Calcifications were present within the lesion (Figures 1b and 3). A focal dehiscence of the sinus roof in close proximity to the anterior cranial fossa floor and sclerosis of the posterior and lateral walls of the sinus were seen (Figure 1b and 1c).
 
 On MRI, the lesion was iso to mildly hypointense on T1-W (Figure 2a) and markedly hypointense on T2-W (Figure 2b). Restricted diffusion was observed in the right sphenoid sinus on DWI/ADC (Figure 4a). Post-contrast T1-W imaging revealed a smooth peripheral rim of enhancement (Figure 5). Additionally, a thin dural enhancement was noted contiguous to the erosion of the sinus roof. The adjacent brain parenchyma appeared normal, with no signs of oedema or infiltration.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 32-year-old, 12-week postpartum female presented with an incidental finding of bilaterally enlarged ovaries on ultrasound. The patient was asymptomatic at the time of presentation, with no associated abdominal pain, fever, or systemic symptoms. There was no significant personal or family history of malignancy or other chronic illnesses.
 Imaging Findings: On the initial ultrasound, both ovaries were massively enlarged (right 444 cm³, left 120 cm³) and displayed a heterogeneous echo texture with hypervascularity. No significant free fluid or adnexal torsion was noted.
 
 On contrast-enhanced whole-body MRI, bilateral ovarian masses exhibited scattered bulky lesions with significant diffusion restriction and contrast enhancement, without signs of necrosis or haemorrhage (Figure 1). Additional bulky lesions were identified in the breasts, mesentery, intestinal serosa, peritoneum, and the wall of the terminal ileum.
 
 PET-CT demonstrated intense fluorodeoxyglucose (FDG) uptake (SUV max. 14 g/ml) in the same regions identified on MRI, confirming metabolic activity consistent with malignancy (Figure 2).
 
 Breast imaging (ultrasound and mammography) revealed numerous sharply circumscribed, hypervascular, hypoechoic masses in both breasts (Figure 3). The biggest lesion measured 140 cm³. Mammography confirmed these findings, identifying well-defined, radiodense masses without calcifications or architectural distortion (Figures 4a and 4b).
 
 Taking account of all the imaging features together, these lesions were categorised as BIRADS 5.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 9-year-old child presented with a complaint of intermittent lower abdominal pain and nocturnal enuresis for two months. There were no gastrointestinal issues, weight loss, asthenia, or lower urinary tract symptoms. The patient had no significant past medical history. During the physical examination, a palpable mass with a hard consistency and non-mobile nature was detected on the left flank. Laboratory studies were normal, including urine catecholamine levels. Tumour markers, including hCG, AFP, and CEA, were also assessed and found to be within normal ranges.
 Imaging Findings: Abdominal ultrasound revealed a solid mass inferior to the left kidney and anterior to the psoas muscle (Figure 1a). The mass was vascularised (Figure 1b), had smooth edges, was slightly heterogeneous, predominantly isoechogenic, with some echogenic foci inside, and measured 5 x 5 x 11 cm in the axial, anteroposterior, and longitudinal axes (Figure 1c).
 
 Further MRI studies (Figures 2a and 2b) showed a retroperitoneal mass inferior to the left kidney and between the left psoas and quadratus lumborum muscles. While there was no evidence of invasion into adjacent structures, it did result in anterior shifting of the psoas muscle. The lesion exhibited an intermediate and heterogeneous signal on T2-weighted images and a low signal on T1-weighted images (Figure 3), with no restriction on the diffusion study (Figures 4a and 4b). The dynamic study (Figures 5a, 5b, 5c, and 5d) presented heterogeneous and progressive uptake by the contrast product, being more evident in the later phase.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 39-year-old male, with no prior relevant medical history, presents with atraumatic intermittent sharp pain in the lower segment of the right anterior thoracic wall. The symptoms began two months earlier when the patient started exercising (CrossFit), mostly triggered by training (especially abdominal crunches) whilst being asymptomatic in normal daily activities.
 Imaging Findings: Ultrasound imaging of the costochondral region of the patient showed a normal arrangement of ribs and rib cartilage while the patient was in a supine position (Figure 1). No abnormalities in bone or cartilage contours, nor any soft tissue lesions were identified.
 
 Dynamic ultrasound of the symptomatic area, performed while the patient replicated the movement that elicits pain (abdominal crunches), revealed hypermobility of the 9th rib cartilage, which moved upwards and became pinned beneath the 8th rib cartilage (Figures 2 and 3). This triggered the pain, with a popping sensation associated. When the patient returned to the supine position, the 9th rib returned to its normal position (Figure 4).

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 25-year-old female presents to the emergency department with disorientation. No neurological focality is observed. The patient has a history of migraine and blurred vision in the past year. No other relevant medical background.
 Imaging Findings: CT shows loss of grey-white matter differentiation in the left parietal lobe and hypointensity in the white matter (Figures 1a and 1b), which indicates a stroke lesion in the left parietal lobe. In CT with contrast (Figures 2a and 2b), stenosis of the right middle cerebral artery (arrow), a filiform course of the left middle cerebral artery and extensive collateral circulation in the region are seen (arrowheads).
 
 Angiogram of the left cerebral circulation (Figures 3a and 3b) confirms the presence of significant vascular collateralisation around the circle of Willis (arrows), giving the classic “puff of smoke” appearance. An irregular vascular course with multiple stenoses is also observed. The angiogram of the contralateral side (Figures 3c and 3d) shows similar findings.
 
 FLAIR-weighted (Figure 4a) and T1 post-contrast (Figure 4b) images show cortical hyperintensity and cortical enhancement, respectively, in the left parietal region (arrows).

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 47-year-old man presented with a history of weakness in the right arm since a few weeks. There was a history of a road traffic accident two months back.
 Imaging Findings: MRI of the brachial plexus showed a pre-fixed plexus with supply from the C4 nerve root. There was diffuse oedema in the C4–C6 nerve roots with thickening of the C5 and C6 nerve roots and the superior trunk (Figures 1a, 1b, and 2c). The nerve roots were hyperintense on T2 sequences and bulky as compared to the contralateral side (Figures 2a and 2b). There was oedematous thickening of the right axillary nerve throughout its course with subacute denervation oedema in the deltoid and the teres minor muscles (Figures 3a, 3b, and 3c). Subacute denervation with grade I fatty infiltration is also noted in the supraspinatus and infraspinatus muscles.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 68-year-old male presented with a gradually enlarging swelling over the anterior aspect of the right ankle, first noticed two years earlier. He reported no pain, trauma, functional limitation, or gait disturbance. On examination, a ~5 cm, well-circumscribed, mobile, and non-tender soft tissue mass was observed, not adherent to deep planes and without skin changes or signs of inflammation.
 Imaging Findings: Ultrasound demonstrated a well-circumscribed soft tissue mass measuring 33 × 28 × 14 mm (L × AP × T) within the subcutaneous tissue, completely enveloping the tibialis anterior tendon. The lesion exhibited a heterogeneous echotexture, predominantly iso-echoic relative to the surrounding fat (Figures 1 and 2).
 
 Subsequent MRI revealed a fusiform, encapsulated mass located within the subcutaneous plane, anterior to the aponeurosis, and circumferentially enveloping the tibialis anterior tendon (Figure 3). The lesion appeared hyperintense on T1-weighted sequences with multiple internal hypointense septations (Figure 3), showed high-signal intensity on T2-weighted gradient-echo sequences (Figure 4), and demonstrated marked hypointensity on fat-suppressed proton density-weighted images (Figures 5 and 6). Across all sequences, the signal characteristics closely resembled those of subcutaneous adipose tissue, consistent with a lipomatous composition.

Clinical History: An 88-year-old patient presented with an incidental lung mass. A computed tomography-guided biopsy and a bronchoscopy were performed, but the results were inconclusive. Given the size and characteristics of the lesion, radiotherapy was considered. The patient was asymptomatic and exhibited a normal clinical examination. The third biopsy yielded diagnostic results.
 Imaging Findings: Conventional chest X-ray shows an enlarged right pulmonary hilum and magnification of the cardiac silhouette due to the superimposed right lower lobe mass (silhouette sign) (Figure 1).
 
 Computed tomography (CT) with a soft tissue window shows a right lower lobe mass with an approximate diameter of 38 x 36 x 66 mm, with ill-defined borders and calcifications within (Figure 2). The pulmonary window shows atelectasis adjacent to the mass, as well as bronchial contact without complete collapse (Figure 4). PET/CT (positron emission tomography) shows FDG (fluorodeoxyglucose) fixation in the lesion, suggesting malignancy (Figure 3).

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 52-year-old woman presented to the emergency department with abdominal pain. The patient had a history of a complicated appendectomy several years prior. Surgeons requested a contrast-enhanced CT scan to clarify the diagnosis.
 Imaging Findings: The contrast-enhanced CT scan revealed dilated small bowel loops with an apparent calibre change in the lower quadrant of the abdomen (Figure 1). As no masses or bowel thickening were identified, the diagnosis was consistent with an incomplete small bowel obstruction, likely due to abdominal adhesions—a diagnosis of exclusion. The patient had a history of a complicated appendectomy several years prior.
 
 Additionally, the gastric wall showed thickened gastric folds (Figure 2). Subsequent gastroscopy confirmed the thickened folds, and biopsies were obtained (Figures 3a, 3b, and 3c). Histopathological examination revealed atrophic gastritis and tumoural cells positive for GATA 3, mammaglobin, and oestrogen receptors.
 
 Both conventional (Figures 4a and 4b) and contrast-enhanced mammography (Figures 5a and 5b) demonstrated bilateral diffuse masses with non-mass enhancement. Palpable areas were biopsied in the lower inner quadrant of the right breast—4 o’clock—(Figure 6a) and the junction of the outer quadrants of the left breast—9 o’clock—(Figure 6b). No axillary lymphadenopathy was detected (Figures 6c and 6d).

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 13-year-old female came with complaints of a slowly growing suprapubic mass since a few months and lower abdominal discomfort. She was further advised to undergo ultrasound imaging and subsequently CT abdomen for renal anomaly, and MRI pelvis for further evaluation.
 Imaging Findings: MRI pelvis reveals a large tubular T1 hyperintense structure in the pelvis on the right side, consisting of haemorrhagic contents in an enlarged hourglass-shaped right uterine horn and cervix. The left uterine horn appears unremarkable. CT abdomen (Figures 1 and 2) reveals an empty right renal fossa with a cross-fused kidney seen on the left. No obvious free fluid or adnexal lesion is seen.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 34-year-old male with one week of sudden-onset fever, abdominal pain, cervicalgia, myalgia, palpitations and shortness of breath. Recent history of travel to Africa for 1 month, and medications for malaria prophylaxis and pre-exposure prophylaxis for HIV. On examination, he had a fever of 38,2°C, tachycardia and right lower quadrant abdominal tenderness. Initial workup was non-contributory, with normal CBC, BMP, chest x-ray, and abdomen ultrasound. With wide differentials, including endemic, infectious and neoplastic conditions, CT of the head, neck and chest was performed and was unrevealing. Serology and lumbar puncture were also performed and were unremarkable.
 Imaging Findings: A contrast-enhanced CT of the abdomen and pelvis was performed, and revealed a 3.7 x 3.1 x 5.1 cm, avidly enhancing paraduodenal mass with central calcification (Figures 1a and 1b), presumed to be an enlarged lymph node. Adjacent organs, including the bowel, were unremarkable. There was no lymphadenopathy elsewhere. An endoscopic ultrasound was done (Figures 2a and 2b) to obtain an aspiration biopsy that revealed B-cell follicles with no evidence of malignancy. After discussion, the patient opted for watchful waiting. A follow-up CT done after 4 months demonstrated no significant change in size or appearance of the mass. This was subsequently excised, and histopathology revealed the final diagnosis.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 62-year-old woman with a 17-year history of diabetes mellitus presented to our institution with a 3-month history of progressive lower urinary tract symptoms, including urinary frequency, urgency, dysuria, and gross haematuria. Notably absent was acute right lower quadrant abdominal pain. After hospitalisation, it was found that there were pneumaturia and faeces-like changes in the urine (Figure 1).
 Imaging Findings: Transabdominal ultrasonography showed a gas-like strong echo and irregular hyperechoic stone-like mass with posterior shadowing in the bladder (Figure 2a). To exclude gynaecological diseases, a transvaginal ultrasound was performed. The right lateral wall of the bladder and the adjacent bowel were connected through a fistula. The fistula tract was about 3.8 cm long and 0.58 cm wide, characterised as a central hyperechoic and peripheral hypoechoic tract on sonogram (Figure 2b). These ultrasound findings supported the diagnosis of vesico-enteric fistula, but the specific part of the intestine could not be clarified.
 
 A computed tomography (CT) scan showed a small air-fluid level at the top of the bladder (Figure 3a). A tubular tract could be seen protruding from the right lateral wall of the bladder up to the neighbouring thickened bowel and this is filled with contrast-opacified urine (Figure 3b). A diagnosis of fistulous communication between the bladder and bowel was suggested following the findings on the excretory-phase CT scan. CT coronal scan showed faecal stones in the bladder (Figure 3c).
 
 Cystoscopy showed an oedematous right lateral wall of the bladder associated with inflammatory changes. It also revealed a fistulous tract opening with a diameter of 1 cm, with pus moss and yellow floc attached to the bladder mucosal surface. The yellow floc is the stone-like mass seen on the ultrasound. The cystoscope could be inserted via a sinus tract opening in the right lateral bladder wall, where the distal tip exhibited bifurcation. Despite attempts, the endoscope could only advance 2 cm beyond the entry point, with further progression prevented by this structural anomaly.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 68-year-old former smoker with hypertension, type 2 diabetes, and hypothyroidism consulted for progressive exertional dyspnoea. Pulmonary function tests showed a restrictive pattern and reduced diffusing capacity of the lung for carbon monoxide (DLCO).
 Imaging Findings: High-resolution computed tomography (HRCT) of the chest with axial (Figures 1a and 1b), coronal (Figure 1c), and sagittal (Figure 1d) reconstructions demonstrated findings consistent with an interstitial lung disease. Bilateral involvement with a clear apicobasal gradient with extensive subpleural reticulation, traction bronchiectasis, and predominant honeycombing in the lower lobes was observed. Exuberant honeycombing was present, involving nearly the entire fibrotic pulmonary parenchyma (Figure 1b). A sharp and abrupt transition between normal and fibrotic lung parenchyma was noted in the craniocaudal direction (arrows in Figures 1c and 1d). The small areas of fibrosis of the upper lobes were limited to the anterior segments (arrowheads in Figures 1a and 1d).
 
 No evidence of superimposed infection or ground-glass opacities was revealed. No suspicious pulmonary nodules, masses, or mediastinal lymphadenopathy were identified. Mild dilation of the pulmonary arteries and a cardiac pacemaker were noted. No pleural or pericardial effusion was present.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 67-year-old woman with a longstanding history of rheumatoid factor-positive rheumatoid arthritis (RA) was being treated with subcutaneous methotrexate 10 mg. She went to the emergency department due to sudden dyspnoea and left chest pain.
 Imaging Findings: Chest X-ray showed a moderate left pneumothorax and a cavitated lesion in the subpleural region of the left upper lobe (LUL) (Figure 1a), which was present on a prior chest X-ray (Figure 1b). After a chest tube was placed (Figure 1c), the pneumothorax did not fully resolve, and symptoms persisted, prompting a CT scan.
 
 CT reported a left pneumothorax. The tube was correctly positioned. A cavitated nodule located in the LUL with communication to the pleural cavity was shown in axial (Figure 2a) and coronal planes (Figure 2b). Other broad-based nodules in the pleural surface were identified (Figure 2c). The rest of the parenchyma presented a slight thickening of the interlobular septa with a diffuse bilateral distribution, probably related to RA-associated interstitial lung disease.
 
 A subsequent revaluation, after reviewing the patient’s radiological history, concluded that the image of a cavitated nodule (Figures 2a and 2b) corresponded to the lesion visible in the previous X-ray (Figure 1b), and proposed the differential diagnosis between cavitated lung neoplasia, rheumatoid nodule and other cavitary lesions such as tuberculosis.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 62-year-old male patient presented to the emergency room with visual disturbances and right-hand weakness. He had a medical history of alcoholism, tobacco use, hyperlipidaemia, and traumatic splenectomy, and was hospitalised due to an ischemic stroke. He underwent routine diagnostics, where, from laboratory tests, only hyperlipidaemia was clinically significant.
 Imaging Findings: Routine abdominal ultrasound revealed an asymptomatic, well-defined, homogeneous, hypoechogenic mass measuring 46 x 36 mm in the 8th liver segment (Figure 1).
 
 On abdominal multidetector computerised tomography (MDCT), the mass was ill-defined and isodense to the surrounding liver parenchyma on unenhanced scans (Figure 2a). Following intravenous contrast, the lesion demonstrated increased attenuation (Figure 2b), whereas, in the latter phases, it was isodense with the surrounding liver parenchyma (Figure 2c). Other findings included two splenunculi, one near the lesser curvature of the stomach (Figure 2d) and one in the left subphrenic region (Figure 2e).
 
 Contrast abdominal MR scans confirmed a well-defined, slightly T2-hyperintense mass measuring 43 x 30 mm (Figure 3a). The mass showed poor uniform post-contrast enhancement in the arterial phase (Figure 3b), following washout in later phases (Figure 3c). The mass showed significant restricted diffusion on DWI (Figure 3d) and low ADC values (Figure 3e).

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 36-year-old male with chronic alcohol use presented with a sudden loss of consciousness. He had no history of seizures, fever, or vomiting. Blood tests were normal. Vitamin B12 and folate levels were not measured.
 Imaging Findings: Brain MRI showed:
 
 Symmetrical T2 and FLAIR hyperintensities in corpus callosum (Figures 1a, 1b, 2a, 2b, 3a, 3b, 4a, 4b, and 5) with T1 hypointensities (Figures 1c, 2c, 3c, 4c, and 4d).
 
 Diffusion restriction on DWI with low ADC values (Figures 1d, 1e, 2d, 2e, 3d, 3e, 4d, and 4e).
 
 Additional involvement of the bilateral fronto-parieto-temporal cortex and corticospinal tracts involving the posterior limb of the internal capsule and midbrain.
 
 No post-contrast enhancement (Figures 6a and 6b).
PostDescription:
Clinical History: A 36-year-old male with chronic alcohol use presented with a sudden loss of consciousness. He had no history of seizures, fever, or vomiting. Blood tests were normal. Vitamin B12 and folate levels were not measured.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 64-year-old man with a history of smoking and occasional alcohol use presented with a foreign body sensation and odynophagia. Clinical examination showed a bulge on the right lateral pharyngeal wall with normal mucosa and palatine tonsil. Neurological exam and lab tests, particularly white blood cell count, SR and RCP, were unremarkable.
 Imaging Findings: The patient underwent an MR scan showing a 20 x 35 x 43.5 mm cystic mass in the right retropharyngeal space (RPS) (Figures 1a and 1b), bulging the prevertebral muscles and impinging on the oropharynx, with leftward deviation and reduced airway patency. The mass was spontaneously hyperintense to muscle on T1W images (Figure 2), hyperintense on T2W images, with a thin septation on T2 FS images (Figure 3). There was no restricted diffusion on the ADC map (Figure 4), and no enhancement was noted on subtracted T1W images before and after gadolinium administration (Figures 5a and 5b). There was no oedema in the surrounding tissues, and no abnormalities were seen in the pharynx, larynx, oesophagus, thyroid, or cervical lymph nodes.
 
 A comparison with an MR scan from 20 years ago showed the lesion was already present but smaller (10 x 20 x 35 mm) (Figure 6). At that time, the MRI was performed for unrelated reasons, and the incidental retropharyngeal lesion was interpreted as a presumed vascular malformation. Since the patient was asymptomatic, follow-up observation and monitoring was implemented, but the patient was lost to follow-up after a few years.

Clinical History: A sixteen-year-old male patient presented with congenital deafness on the left side and normal hearing on the right side. The thin, heavily T2-weighted images revealed a bud-like, cystic cochlea on the left side with absent normal turns, modiolus and interscalar septa (Figures 1a, 1b, 1c and 6). The vestibular nerve did not bifurcate into a superior and inferior branch. The left inner ear canal was small in calibre as well, progressively narrowing from medial to lateral. The left vestibule appeared mildly globose (Figure 2). The left lateral semicircular canal exhibited focal signal loss, which could either be a true abnormality or an artefact. The inner ear structures and inner ear canal on the right side were normally developed (Figures 1a, 1b, 3b, 5a, 5b, 5c, 5d, 5e, and 5f).

Clinical History: A 29-year-old male with a history of recurrent urolithiasis presented with persistent abdominal pain and unexplained fever. Blood tests revealed elevated inflammatory markers and impaired renal function.
 Imaging Findings: On the CT scan, the left kidney is markedly enlarged, and multiple kidney calculi, including a staghorn calculus, are identified within the calyces and renal pelvis (Figures 1a and 2a). Diffuse thinning of the renal cortex and non-homogeneous, poorly differentiated renal parenchyma, replaced by multiple hypodense fluid-filled cavities (mean attenuation ~ +11 HU) (Figure 1b), with rim enhancement (Figure 3), consistent with the “bear paw sign”, associated with minimal air-fluid levels. Fat-density areas (mean attenuation ~ -60 HU) within and adjacent to the fluid collections (xanthomas) are seen (Figure 1c). The renal pelvis and calyces are dilated. The perirenal fat stranding and thickening of both Gerota’s and Zuckerkandl’s fasciae are noted, indicating stage II–III inflammatory extension. The entire ureter shows diffuse wall thickening with fat stranding and linear calcifications in the mid-ureter (Figure 2b). On the delayed urographic phase, there is no contrast excretion, confirming impaired left kidney function (Figure 4a and 4b).

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 29-year-old female patient presented to the cardiology department with episodes of numbness in both her upper and lower extremities, which spontaneously resolved after 30 minutes. She also reported episodes of chest pain that she attributed to stress. Heart auscultation was normal. ECG showed diffuse ST-elevations.
 Imaging Findings: Echocardiography (ECG) demonstrated severe systolic heart failure, with a left ventricular ejection fraction (LVEF) of 30%. The exam showed basal hypokinesia and preserved apical contractility, along with irregularities in the lining of the left ventricular myocardium (Figure 1).
 
 A contrast-enhanced computed tomography (CECT) scan revealed no coronary abnormalities but confirmed the irregularities in the left ventricle wall (Figure 2).
 
 Subsequently, a cardiac MRI was performed, which revealed moderate left ventricle dilation and irregularities in the ventricle wall, including protrusions of myocardial bridges, multiple crypts, and thinning of the inferolateral apex with diffuse dyskinesia (Figures 3 and 4). Severe left ventricular dysfunction was confirmed, with an LVEF of 20% and a global longitudinal strain (GLS) of -7.85%, as well as ventricular dyssynchrony. A non-ischemic fibrosis region was also present in the interventricular septum and the posterior hinge point. The right ventricle appeared to be normal in morphology and function.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 2-year-old male child was brought by his 34-year-old mother with complaints of abdominal pain and recurrent vomiting. The child was clinically stable, and the abdominal ultrasound was normal. However, we incidentally discovered that both the mother and the son had physical impairments in their bilateral upper and lower limbs.
 Imaging Findings: The child had a classical median cleft with rudimentary bilateral thumbs with loss of opposition movement (Figure 1). A frontal radiograph revealed a bilateral median cleft, and the right hand’s proximal phalanges of the middle and index fingers fused, resulting in syndactyly. An additional middle phalanx was observed in the right thumb. Bilaterally, the thumb and index finger were displaced toward each other (Figures 2a and 2b). The radiography of the child’s feet depicted the absence of three of his metatarsals (Figure 2c). The X-ray of the infant’s skull showed multiple convolutions, suggesting a copper-beaten appearance (Figure 3).
 
 Interestingly, his 34-year-old mother, too, suffered from a similar deformity. On clinical inspection, both her right and left hands showed the presence of only three fingers (Figure 4a). A hypoplastic index finger was seen in the left hand. Bilateral feet showed only two toes with possible syndactyly (Figure 4b).

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 33-year-old female presents with a 6-year history of abnormal involuntary movements of all limbs, face, and lips, which initially started as increased fidgeting and finger flicking. Cognitive decline began a year later, with memory loss, disorientation, and difficulty dressing and eating, and now is fully dependent for activities of daily living.
 Imaging Findings: A contrast-enhanced MRI (CEMRI) of the brain was performed with 1.5T with sequences of T1WI inversion recovery 3D, T2WI axial and coronal, 3D FLAIR, SWI, DWI, and MR spectroscopy (MRS). MRI revealed atrophy of the head of the caudate bilaterally (Figures 1 and 2) with decreased FH:CC ratio of 1.18 (frontal horn width to intercaudate distance ratio) (Figure 5) and increased CC:IT ratio of 0.25 (intercaudate distance to inner table width ratio) (Figure 6). Volume loss of the putamen and globus pallidus was noted bilaterally. An enlargement of the bilateral frontal horns of the lateral ventricles, giving a box-like configuration, is noted. There is evidence of T2/FLAIR hyperintensities (Figures 3 and 4) seen in the involved region with mild grade II mineralisation seen in globi pallidi, extending to bilateral posterolateral putamen and also in the substantia nigra. Mild prominence of sulci and ventricles with grey matter volume loss is suggestive of mild cerebral volume loss. MRS shows choline peak and mild NAA (N-acetyl aspartate) loss in the basal ganglia (Figures 7a, 7b, 7c, and 7d).

No blooming artefacts on the gradient echo sequences (GRE) images (Figure 4). No evidence of true diffusion restriction. OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 56-year-old woman presented with a painless right breast lump, present for three months. She also reported easy fatigability, ptosis, and exertional dyspnoea for one year. She was diagnosed with myasthenia gravis, and was started on immunosuppressants five months ago. There was no history of tuberculosis (TB) or contact with TB patients.
 Imaging Findings: High-resolution CT of the thorax showed a well-defined soft tissue density in the anterior superior mediastinum. CT-guided biopsy revealed polygonal epithelial cells with interspersed lymphocytes, suggestive of thymoma type B1. Only non-contrast-enhanced CT of the thorax was performed (Figures 1a, 1b, and 1c) due to contraindication to iodinated contrast in myasthenia gravis. An elliptical pleural-based hyperdense lesion with peripheral calcification and central fluid density was noted along the lateral right hemithorax, suggestive of empyema. A lobulated soft tissue lesion was seen in the lower inner quadrant of the right breast, extending into the underlying pectoral muscle. Ultrasound correlation was advised.
 
 Ultrasound of the right breast (Figures 2a, 2b, and 3) revealed an irregular, heterogeneously hypoechoic lesion with posterior enhancement, oriented parallel to the parenchyma from 3 to 5 o’clock position, 2 cm from the nipple–areola complex. No internal vascularity or calcification was seen. The lesion extended into the pectoralis muscle and appeared soft on elastography. It was categorised as BIRADS 4A.
 
 Fine-needle aspiration cytology was advised (Figures 4a and 4b).

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: An 18-year-old female who recently migrated with her family due to war presented with longstanding, progressive motor impairment of unclear origin. She was referred by an outpatient neurologist after multiple sclerosis had been excluded. No previous medical records were available from her country of origin.
 
 According to her mother, she developed normally in her early infancy, walking independently. Around 17–18 months, shortly after vaccination (possibly MMR), she developed dragging of one leg, recurrent fevers, and loss of motor milestones. A verbal diagnosis of post-viral encephalitis was given at the time. Her condition fluctuated, but cognition remained intact. She developed progressive gait decline during her childhood. Wheelchair use began at age 15, and she is now fully dependent for transfers, though she eats and writes independently.
 
 Clinical and neurological examination revealed esophoria, mild tongue deviation, weak facial muscles, hypotrophic upper extremities with reduced fine motor control, joint hypermobility, increased tone and reflexes with bilateral positive Trömner signs, and severe leg-dominant spastic tetraparesis.
 
 Given the unclear clinical presentation, brain and spinal MRI were performed.
 Imaging Findings: Brain MRI revealed symmetrical T2 (Figures 1) and FLAIR (Figures 2) hyperintensities in the supratentorial white matter (excluding U-fibres), splenium of the corpus callosum, posterior limb of the internal capsule, pons along trigeminal pathways, inferior cerebellar peduncles, and pyramidal tracts of the medulla oblongata. DWI (Figures 3) showed restricted diffusion with low ADC (Figures 4). No contrast enhancement was observed (Figures 5). MR spectroscopy (Figures 6) revealed a lactate peak at the level of the abnormal white matter. Spinal MRI (Figures 7 and 8) displayed longitudinal signal abnormalities predominantly involving the dorsal columns and lateral corticospinal tracts throughout the entire cord. Findings suggested a metabolic leukodystrophy. Diagnosis was confirmed by genetic testing.

Clinical History: An 81-year-old man was referred with a four-month history of persistent right upper quadrant pain radiating to the back. Liver function tests were normal, and he denied fever or pruritus. His medical history included pacemaker implantation, malaria, appendicectomy, arterial hypertension, and orthopaedic surgeries. No alcohol abuse or smoking was reported.
 Imaging Findings: The patient first underwent abdominal ultrasound and contrast-enhanced CT scan (Figures 1a and 1b), which revealed consistent findings: gallbladder hydrops with thin walls, moderate dilatation of the intrahepatic right bile ducts, and an ill-defined 1.5 cm soft-tissue lesion at the junction of the cystic duct and the common bile duct.
 
 Gadoxetic acid-enhanced MRI (Figures 2a and 2b) with magnetic resonance cholangiopancreatography (MRCP) (Figures 3a and 3b) confirmed tissue infiltration at the junction of the right anterior segmental duct and common bile duct, explaining the dilatation of the right anterior bile ducts.
 
 Endoscopic ultrasound and endoscopic retrograde cholangiopancreatography (ERCP) (Figure 4) were subsequently performed to perform biopsies and to place a plastic biliary stent.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 4-month-old male child from a tuberculosis (TB) endemic region presented with complaints of poor feeding, loss of weight for a month, and a low-grade, evening fever without any associated rash or chills for 15 days. The patient also had jerky movements of the right upper and lower limbs without any other signs of seizure disorder.
 
 The patient showed positive Kernig’s (pain and resistance to knee extension on flexing the patient’s hip and knee to 90 degrees and then attempting to extend the knee) [1] and Brudziński’s signs (in a patient with supine position, positive Brudziński’s is involuntary flexion of the hips and knees when the patient’s neck is passively flexed) [2].
 Imaging Findings: As the patient showed positive Kernig’s and Brudziński’s signs, meningeal TB was suspected. However, the chest radiograph was normal. On MRI, subtle T2 FLAIR (fluid attenuation inversion recovery) hyperintense minimally enhancing foci were seen in the bilateral cerebral cortex, caudate nucleus, thalami, and right hemipons with diffusion restriction and without blooming on gradient echo (GRE) sequences (Figures 1a and 1b). Subtle areas of leptomeningeal enhancement along the bilateral frontoparietal region were also seen. Mild communicating hydrocephalus was seen.
 
 A follow-up scan was advised after 4 months of antituberculosis treatment (ATT), as the patient had reduced activity, drowsiness, and episodes of seizure. On follow-up MRI, multiple conglomerated T2 hyperintense T1 fat-sat ring-enhancing lesions were seen in bilateral gangliocapsular and periventricular regions with leptomeningeal enhancement and hydrocephalus (Figures 2a and 2b). Mild surrounding cerebral oedema was also noted.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: An eight-month-old female child presented with jaundice and abdominal distension. Clinical examination revealed icterus, a distended stomach, and prominent superficial veins. Laboratory reports showed elevated serum glutamic pyruvic transaminase and serum glutamic oxaloacetic transaminase levels with conjugated hyperbilirubinemia.
 Imaging Findings: Ultrasound (USG) showed grossly coarsened echotexture of liver with multiple tiny communicating and non-communicating cystic lesions (Figures 1a and 1c). A slightly irregular liver outline was also noted (Figure 1c), indicating liver cirrhosis. Figure 1b shows a large intrahepatic cyst near the porta hepatis containing echogenic debris in dependent location, measuring 2.2 x 1.1 cm (indicating Caroli disease). Many sections also showed ill-defined hypoechoic nodules in the liver, indicative of dysplastic nodules (Figure 1d). The gallbladder was distended, showing an elongated gallbladder neck with a thickened wall (2.4 mm) and increased pericholecystic echogenicity (Figure 2a). Mild dilatation of the cystic duct was also noted (Figure 2b). Another section showed both liver and right kidney in profile with increased renal parenchymal echotexture (Figure 3). Colour Doppler assessment showed absent colour flow in cystic structures, suggesting cystic lesions to be biliary dilatations (Figures 4a and 4b). A splenomegaly of 8 cm with a dilated (10 mm) splenic vein was noted (Figure 6).

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: An 11-year-old girl with normal hearing and cognition had severe dysarthria and language delay since birth. Neurological exam showed no motor deficits but significant expressive impairment. Given the persistent speech delay, a brain MRI was requested for further evaluation.

There is marked hypointensity of the globus pallidus on T2-weighted and FLAIR sequences secondary to iron deposition, containing minimal dot-like hyperintensities (vacuolisation), associated with comma-shaped high-signal intensity in the antero-medial part of the globus pallidus, likely due to gliosis (Figures 1 and 2). There is also marked hypointensity on both T2*EG and DWI (diffusion-weighted images) sequences due to susceptibility effects (Figures 3 and 4).

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A nine-year-old boy, previously healthy, presented with fever, cough, dyspnoea, vomiting and diarrhoea. Eighteen days after hospital admission, he had the first spontaneous bilateral pneumothorax, needing drainage, intensive care unit admission, and mechanical ventilation. After 3 months, even with remaining lung lesions and pneumothorax, he could be discharged for outpatient follow-up.
 Imaging Findings: The initial imaging exam was a chest radiograph (X-ray) showing a bilateral diffuse micronodular acinar infiltrate (Figures 1a and 1b). A computed tomography (CT) was performed, which revealed a diffuse micronodular pattern and random distribution throughout the lung parenchyma, suggestive of an inflammatory/infectious process (Figures 2a, 2b, and 2c).
 
 The patient experienced several pneumothoraces, all of which were drained. Up to three chest tubes were placed simultaneously (Figures 3a, 3b, 3c, 3d, 3e, and 3f). A second contrast-enhanced chest CT revealed confluent cystic lesions, prevailing on the upper lobes and posterior regions (Figures 4a, 4b, 4c, 4d, 4e, and 4f).
 
 A few weeks later, another contrast-enhanced chest CT showed the same findings, but with larger pneumothoraces on both sides, despite the presence of three thoracic drains (Figures 5a, 5b, 5c, 5d, and 5e). Pneumomediastinum was noted.
 
 Before hospital discharge, the patient underwent a new contrast-enhanced chest CT. The lung abnormalities persisted, but the patient was able to tolerate them even after the chest tubes were removed (Figures 6a, 6b, and 6c).
 
 Eight months later, a follow-up chest CT revealed almost complete recovery of the pulmonary abnormalities, with sequelae changes and discreet cystic lesions, and with favourable evolution of the imaging findings over the eleven-month follow-up period (Figures 7a, 7b, and 7c).

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 73-year-old female patient underwent follow-up at our institution for 14 years due to a pancreatic head lesion. The lesion was monitored at six-month intervals through ultrasound, computed tomography, or magnetic resonance imaging, revealing sustained growth throughout the surveillance period. However, the patient remained asymptomatic, and laboratory tests were unremarkable.
 Imaging Findings: Cross-sectional imaging of the upper abdomen, namely computed tomography (CT) and magnetic resonance imaging (MRI), revealed a multicystic lesion in the pancreatic head, with lobulated margins, enhancing internal septations (Figures 1, 2, and 3) and a central fibrotic scar with stellate calcifications (Figures 1a–1h). On ultrasound, the lesion depicts multiple hypoechoic/anechoic cysts and hyperechoic septations (Figure 4).
 
 Over time, the lesion’s enlargement led to gradual dilatation of both the bile ducts and the main pancreatic duct. In the most recent magnetic resonance cholangiopancreatography (MRCP) (Figures 2a–2i), the pancreatic lesion measured approximately 97 x 86 x 126 mm (length x width x anteroposterior axis). The common bile duct had a calibre of 15 mm, and the main pancreatic duct had a maximum calibre of 7 mm. The pancreatic parenchyma was atrophic.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 78-year-old female presented to the surgery outpatient department with complaints of painless swelling, posterior to the left knee, for 20 years, with a recent onset of pain (since 3 months) after getting hit by an animal at the site of swelling. On examination, the swelling was firm and lobulated with no overlying skin changes. There was a history of weight loss and loss of appetite. No other comorbidities. No family history of malignancy.
 Imaging Findings: A plain lateral radiograph of the knee demonstrated a soft tissue lesion seen on the posterior aspect of the knee (Figure 1).
 
 Soft tissue ultrasound (US) demonstrated a large lobulated and irregularly shaped heterogeneously hypoechoic mass lesion with internal vascularity, seen posterior to the left knee (Figure 2 ). A few central hypoechoic necrotic/cystic areas were also seen. The mass was also seen displacing the popliteal vessels superficially.
 
 MRI (with contrast) findings revealed a large T1 heterointense lobulated mass lesion in the popliteal fossa with patchy areas of T1 hyperintensity (Figure 3). It also showed heterointense signal on T2W images (Figure 4). The lesion also showed patchy areas of diffusion restriction on DWI and ADC (Figures 5a and 5b) and heterogeneous enhancement on post-contrast study (Figure 6). It was seen displacing and causing near-complete encasement of the popliteal neurovascular bundle. There was no obvious involvement/infiltration of the overlying skin.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 36-year-old male, HIV-1 positive since age 22 with no follow-up for 4 years, presented with progressive dyspnoea, cough, thoracic pain, and fatigue. Initially treated for presumed community-acquired pneumonia with partial symptom improvement, but later experienced worsening respiratory symptoms. Denied fever or night sweats.
 Imaging Findings: Chest X-ray revealed mediastinal widening, likely due to a mass, accompanied by ill-defined lower lung opacities and bilateral pleural effusion, more pronounced on the right (Figure 1).
 
 Thoracic CT demonstrated a large anterior mediastinal mass (10.8 × 5.3 × 7.6 cm), heterogeneously enhancing with cystic areas and displacement of adjacent vascular structures without invasion, likely representing a conglomerate of necrotic hypervascular lymph nodes (Figure 2). Additional mediastinal adenopathies were noted.
 
 Pulmonary findings included bilateral “flame-shaped” peribronchovascular opacities (Figure 3a), patchy ground-glass opacities, and interlobular septal thickening (Figure 3b). Bilateral pleural effusion was also evident. Systemic hypervascular adenopathies involved the axillary, mesenteric, and inguinal regions bilaterally (short diameter up to 1.7 cm) (Figures 4a, 4b, and 4c).
 
 Abdominal imaging revealed cutaneous millimetric nodules in the thoracic and abdominal walls (Figure 5), hepatomegaly (17.9 cm in craniocaudal length), and gastric wall thickening (Figures 6a and 6b).

Assess each:

In HIV, can have large anterior mediastinal mass due to lymphoma (e.g., primary mediastinal large B-cell lymphoma, also HIV-associated diffuse large B-cell). Usually hyperdense, may have necrosis. Also systemic lymphadenopathy, extranodal involvement (hepatic, gastric). Could cause "lymphangitic carcinomatosis"-like pattern? Could cause peribronchovascular thickening from lymphatic spread. So plausible.

- Atypical pneumonia: would cause ground-glass, consolidations but not large mediastinal mass, systemic adenopathy or cutaneous nodules. So less likely.

- Mycobacterial infection (TB, atypical mycobacteria): can cause mediastinal adenopathy, necrotic nodes, lung infiltrates, pleural effusion, constitutional symptoms. However would not cause hypervascular adenopathy in axillary, inguinal, mesenteric; cutaneous nodules less typical; gastric wall thickening possible (TB). But large contiguous mass 10 cm anterior mediastinum less typical; more commonly conglomerate nodes causing mass effect may be seen but HIV may get disseminated MAC causing adenopathy. Could be but the hypervascular nature is more typical of lymphoma.

- Multicentric Castleman disease (MCD): HIV-associated, HHV-8 driven, presents with systemic lymphadenopathy, fever, constitutional symptoms, may have hypervascular lymph nodes, can involve mediastinum, may have pulmonary infiltrates with “ground glass” and “flame-shaped” opacities (due to lymphatic infiltration). Also can have skin lesions, hepatomegaly, splenomegaly, and systemic symptoms. The CT often shows enhancing lymph nodes with central necrosis? Typically they are hyperenhancing due to vascularity. Also can have “lymphangitic” pulmonary pattern. So MCD is strong.

- Disseminated AIDS-related Kaposi sarcoma: Usually cutaneous lesions (violaceous), visceral involvement including lungs, GI, lymph nodes but lung lesions are nodular, perihilar infiltrates, pleural effusions, often with bilateral peribronchovascular nodules ("flame-shaped") and interstitial infiltrates. Mediastinal mass less typical; KS lesions are vascular, can be hypervascular, but large anterior mediastinal mass is unusual. Also lymphadenopathy can be present but less bulky. Gastric wall thickening can be due to KS. However cutaneous nodules described as "millimetric" but not described as violaceous. KS would have typical skin lesions; not mentioned. Also mass necrotic? Unlikely.

- Lymphangitic carcinomatosis: Usually due to metastatic carcinoma (e.g., adenocarcinoma of stomach, breast, lung). In HIV patient this less likely. Also mass is not typical; may have adenopathy but not hypervascular; also systemic adenopathy beyond thorax less typical.

- Pulmonary oedema: would cause interstitial pattern, but not mass, adenopathy, cutaneous nodules.

- Sarcoidosis: mediastinal lymphadenopathy, lung infiltrates, but nodes are not necrotic, hypervascular mass unlikely, and skin lesions are often papules, not described as millimetric nodules.

Therefore top candidates: Multicentric Castleman disease vs Lymphoma.

Key distinguishing: MCD tends to have systemic hypervascular lymphadenopathy, especially mesenteric, axillary, inguinal, also hepatosplenomegaly, and pulmonary lymphangitic pattern (“flame-shaped”), pleural effusions, and skin lesions. Also mass can be large in mediastinum. Lymphoma can also have similar but usually more aggressive, may have B symptoms (fever, night sweats) - patient denies. Also lymphoma commonly invades structures; here no invasion noted. MCD nodes are usually non-invasive. Also gastric wall thickening can be MCD infiltration.

Thus MCD appears most consistent.

Now evaluate other pros/cons:

Lymphoma pros: large mediastinal mass, hypervascular nodes, HIV predisposition, systemic adenopathy, hepatic involvement. Cons: usually B symptoms (fever, night sweats), may cause more necrosis; but can be asymptomatic; but cutaneous nodules less typical; gastric involvement possible but less common; peribronchovascular "flame-shaped" opacities can be lymphangitic spread but less common in lymphoma.

MCD pros: systemic hypervascular adenopathy in multiple regions, hepatic involvement, gastric wall thickening, cutaneous lesions, bilateral pleural effusion, pulmonary lymphangitic pattern, no invasive mass, typical in HIV patients. Cons: Anterior mediastinal mass as large as 10 cm is less common but possible as conglomerate nodes.

Thus MCD is best.

Therefore final answer: Multicentric Castleman disease.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 19-day-old female newborn was brought to our hospital because of left breast tumefaction for 2 days, with localised erythema and irritability. No fluctuance or nipple discharge was noted. No fever was present. The white blood cell count was elevated. The delivery was eutocic (40 weeks and 2 days) and unremarkable.
 Imaging Findings: The right breast was unremarkable (Figure 1a). The breast bud is hypoechoic relative to subcutaneous fat. A well-defined T1 hyperintense and predominantly T2 hypointense (T2 shading sign) cystic lesion with a thin wall is noted in the right adnexa, arising from the right ovary (Figures 5 and 6). Minimal pelvic ascites is noted.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 22-year-old female presented with dysphagia for 9 years and gradually increasing vulval swelling for 5 years. Clinical examination revealed a firm, non-tender, lobulated mass (~8x8 cm) involving bilateral labia with intact overlying skin. Upper gastrointestinal endoscopy showed a bulge in the mid and lower oesophagus with intact mucosa and luminal narrowing.
 Imaging Findings: MRI thorax showed a long segment bulky circumferential wall thickening of the lower thoracic oesophagus extending till the gastric cardia (Figure 1c), with T1 isointense (Figure 1a) and T2 mildly hyperintense appearance (Figure 1b) compared to the skeletal muscle. It shows mild homogeneous enhancement without necrotic areas (Figure 1d), a very smooth outline and a clear plane with the aorta, cardia and thoracic spine.
 
 MRI pelvis showed a large, lobulated, well-circumscribed mass (~9x7 cm) involving the clitoral region and bilateral labia majora. It appears T1 isointense (Figure 2b) and T2 hyperintense compared to that of skeletal muscle (Figures 2a and 2d), it shows diffusion restriction (Figures 2e and 2f) and a marked homogeneous contrast enhancement (Figure 2c). The mass was seen overhanging the urethral orifice and vaginal introitus without causing urinary outflow obstruction.
 
 Non-contrast PET-CT showed
 
 FDG-avid soft tissue lesions corresponding to the oesophageal wall thickening (SUV max = 9.2) (Figures 1e and 1f) and vulval mass (SUV max = 9.8) (Figures 2g and 2h). There were no calcifications, intralesional fat, or cystic areas within the lesions.

Clinical History: A 6-month-old infant presented with a history of on-and-off soft tissue swelling over the left cheek since 2 months (Figure 1). No history of trauma. Full-term normal delivery.

 On MRI, the corresponding area showed mild STIR hyperintensity (Figure 3) and T1 hypointensity (Figure 4). No evidence of any significant diffusion restriction was seen in this region (Figures 5a and 5b). Surrounding oedematous changes were seen in the left masticator muscles and infratemporal fossa. No focal discrete collection or solid component was seen.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 2-year-old male child was brought by his 34-year-old mother with complaints of abdominal pain and recurrent vomiting. The child was clinically stable, and the abdominal ultrasound was normal. However, we incidentally discovered that both the mother and the son had physical impairments in their bilateral upper and lower limbs.
 Imaging Findings: The child had a classical median cleft with rudimentary bilateral thumbs with loss of opposition movement (Figure 1). A frontal radiograph revealed a bilateral median cleft, and the right hand’s proximal phalanges of the middle and index fingers fused, resulting in syndactyly. An additional middle phalanx was observed in the right thumb. Bilaterally, the thumb and index finger were displaced toward each other (Figures 2a and 2b). The radiography of the child’s feet depicted the absence of three of his metatarsals (Figure 2c). The X-ray of the infant’s skull showed multiple convolutions, suggesting a copper-beaten appearance (Figure 3).
 
 Interestingly, his 34-year-old mother, too, suffered from a similar deformity. On clinical inspection, both her right and left hands showed the presence of only three fingers (Figure 4a). A hypoplastic index finger was seen in the left hand. Bilateral feet showed only two toes with possible syndactyly (Figure 4b).

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 42-year-old male presented to the emergency department with weakness and paraesthesia in the lower limbs, back pain, and fever. Cerebrospinal fluid analysis showed an increased white blood cell count (predominantly polymorphonuclear) and protein. He had a history of gonococcal urethritis and syphilis and a recent influenza B respiratory infection.
 Imaging Findings: Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) shows a longitudinally extensive posterior extradural extramedullary collection from T2 to T9 of heterogeneous appearance with isointense and hyperintense areas in T1-weighted sequence (Figure 1) and high signal intensity on T2-weighted and STIR sequences (Figure 2). It causes a compressive effect on the spinal cord with anterior thecal sac displacement (Figure 3). There is increased STIR and T2 cord signal in the compressed segment, suggestive of compressive myelopathy (Figure 2). The contrast-enhanced T1-weighted sequence shows diffuse meningeal spinal enhancement (Figures 4, 5a and 5b). The magnetic field strength of the MRI scanner used was 3T.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 2-day-old female neonate born through normal vaginal delivery to non-consanguineous marriage was found to have dark pigmentation in the groin, perineum, lower back, upper and lower extremities, including hands, with hair growth in these areas (Figure 1). No other abnormalities on clinical examination and blood workup. This finding raised suspicion of phacomatosis, prompting a brain MRI to assess for neuroparenchymal abnormalities.
 Imaging Findings: Multifocal punctate T1 hyperintense foci scattered in bilateral cerebral cortices, left amygdala and left cerebellar hemisphere (Figures 2a, 2b, and 2c). These did not show restricted diffusion or blooming on SWI. No signal changes were seen on T2 (Figure 3). No leptomeningeal T1 hyperintensities were seen. Post-contrast images showed no leptomeningeal or neuroparenchymal enhancement.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 14-year-old boy presented with a chronic history of rhinosinusitis and productive cough for years. There were no associated symptoms of haemoptysis, fever, or weight loss. On examination, he had mild basal crepitations. Otherwise, no dysmorphic features or dextrocardia. No palpable organomegaly or lymphadenopathy. History of paternal aunt infertility was reported.
 Imaging Findings: Initial chest radiograph showed mild right lower zone faint opacification with no dextrocardia (Figure 1). The high-resolution computed tomography (HRCT) revealed mild bilateral lower lobe predominant bronchiectasis with bronchial wall thickening and mucoid impaction as well as centrilobular nodules (Figures 2a and 2b). The rest of the lung parenchyma was well aerated. No situs inversus or dextrocardia was seen. No mediastinal lymphadenopathy.
 
 Paranasal sinuses (PNS) CT revealed near complete opacification of the paranasal sinuses with frothy secretions as well as associated pharyngitis and left-sided otomastoiditis (Figure 3).

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A previously well 12-year-old male presented to the accident and emergency department of a regional paediatric hospital with a 2-week history of worsening bilateral lower limb paraesthesia in the form of loss of fine touch, vibration and proprioception from the level of T5 distally, signifying mass effect on the dorsal columns with sparing of pain and temperature sensation. He also had reduced motor function of the lower limbs with clonus and brisk reflexes.
 Imaging Findings: MRI brain was normal. MRI of the whole spine (Figures 1a, 1b, 1c, 1d, and 1e) demonstrated a large solitary solid cystic lesion, centred over the spinous process and the lamina of T5 bilaterally, more extensive on the right. The solid lesion is iso/mildly hypointense to bone, with areas of T1 and T2 hypointense signal, suggestive of calcifications. The solid component measured 3.4 x 3.7 x 4.4 cm (transverse x anteroposterior x craniocaudal) and demonstrated avid enhancement. The mass is expansile with cortical expansion and trabecular replacement. It extends into the spinal canal, causing severe canal stenosis and cord compression. There was evidence of bone marrow oedema within the body of T5. No other intra or extramedullary lesions were identified.
 
 A follow-up CT thorax, abdomen and pelvis (Figures 2a and 2b) with bony reformats demonstrated an expansile lytic lesion with cortical expansion involving the posterior elements of T5. There was no bony destruction. There are areas of calcification seen within the internal osseous matrix. No other bony lesions were identified, and no abnormality of note was found in the thorax, abdomen or pelvis.

Similar subcutaneous lesions were noted in bilateral level 4 regions (Figures 1c and 1d), abutting carotid vessels.

Multiple hypodense, hypoenhancing nodules were imaged in the mesentery encasing the inferior mesenteric artery from its origin (Figures 4a and 4b), and extending into the pelvis and bilateral retroperitoneum. Similar nodular lesions were seen extending from the neural foramina of the lumbar spine, extending along the course of nerve roots into sciatica foramina and along the sciatic nerve (Figures 5a, 5b, and 5c). A few small nodules were also noted adjacent to bilateral femoral vessels (Figures 6a and 6b). Multiple variable-sized hypodense subcutaneous nodules were noted diffusely scattered in the abdominal wall.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 59-year-old male agricultural worker from a rural area, with a long history of barefoot fieldwork, presented with a long-standing history of swelling over the plantar surface of the left foot, now progressing to involve the dorsal surface and with a history of a discharging sinus. The patient denies any history of trauma.
 Imaging Findings: A clinical image of the plantar (Figure 1a) and dorsal surface (Figure 1b) of the left foot shows multiple soft and firm nodules that were nontender, along with no discharging sinus at present.
 
 X-ray of the left foot (Figure 2) and lateral views show soft-tissue density over the lateral aspect of the left foot with cortical remodelling of the diaphysis of the 4th metatarsal.
 
 Ultrasound shows multiple cystic lesions, with a few of them showing tiny echogenic grains in the dependent portion (dot in a circle) (arrow in Figure 3).
 
 On T2W sagittal (Figure 4a), soft tissue mass shows conglomerates of small (2–5 mm), rounded, hyperintense lesions with few of them showing central low-signal intensity dots and peripheral low-signal intensity rim around these hyperintense areas, representing a “dot-in-circle” sign. A few cystic areas are noted within this mass, which contains T2 hypointense foci—likely spores. On post-contrast T1 fat-saturated (FS) coronal image (Figure 4b), the involved region of soft tissue shows rim enhancement around the granulation tissue along with minimal enhancement of the central dot. There is sparing of the underlying bones and tendons. No evidence of marrow oedema noted.
